description: Rules ensuring generated games and apps have professional-quality visuals — no plain colored rectangles.

guidelines.rule: NEVER use plain colored rectangles (div with background-color only) for game entities like players, enemies, projectiles, or obstacles. Every entity must have a recognizable shape using SVG, CSS shapes (clip-path, border-radius, box-shadow), pixel-art grid rendering, or canvas drawing.
guidelines.why: Users rated Space Invaders with red rectangles as 'terrible' and refused to publish it. The game was functionally correct but visually unacceptable. Professional-quality sprites are the difference between shareable and shameful.
examples:
BAD:
for an alien
GOOD: renderSprite(ALIEN_1, {1:'#ff0040'}, 4) using pixel grid arrays
GOOD: <svg viewBox='0 0 40 30'><path d='M20,0 L40,30 L0,30 Z' fill='#ff0040'/></svg> for a triangle ship
GOOD: clip-path:polygon(50% 0, 100% 100%, 0 100%) for a simple ship shape

guidelines.rule: Every game must have at least 3 visually distinct entity types. Enemies/aliens should look different from each other (different shapes, colors, or patterns). The player entity must be immediately recognizable and distinct from enemies.
guidelines.why: Games where all entities are the same colored box are boring and confusing. Visual variety is a core quality signal.
examples: Space Invaders: 3 alien types (squid, crab, octopus) + player ship + UFO — each a different shape and color
Breakout: colored bricks (gradient rows), distinct paddle, visible ball with trail

guidelines.rule: Include visual feedback for game events: explosions for destroyed entities, screen shake or flash for impacts, particle effects or trails for movement. Use CSS animations (@keyframes) — they cost zero extra code complexity.
guidelines.why: Static games feel lifeless. Even simple CSS animations dramatically improve perceived quality.
examples: Explosion: @keyframes explode { 0%{transform:scale(1);opacity:1} 100%{transform:scale(2);opacity:0} }
Hit flash: @keyframes flash { 0%,100%{filter:brightness(1)} 50%{filter:brightness(3)} }
Bullet trail: box-shadow:0 0 4px #00ff41, 0 0 8px #00ff41

guidelines.rule: Use the asset pack's renderSprite() function when available. If the arcade-retro asset pack is injected, use its pre-built sprite grids (ALIEN_1, ALIEN_2, ALIEN_3, PLAYER_SHIP, UFO_SHIP) rather than inventing new rectangle-based entities.
guidelines.why: The pre-built sprites are pixel-perfect and tested. LLMs that ignore injected asset packs and fall back to plain rectangles produce the worst visual quality.
examples: const alienHTML = renderSprite(ALIEN_1, {1:'#ff0040'}, 4);
const playerHTML = renderSprite(PLAYER_SHIP, {1:'#00ff41'}, 4);

guidelines.rule: All text must have sufficient contrast against its background. Toasts, tooltips, overlays, and status messages must use HIGH-CONTRAST color pairs: white text (#fff) on dark backgrounds (#000 to #333), or black text (#000) on light backgrounds (#fff to #eee). NEVER use dark gray text (#666 or darker) on gray backgrounds (#888 or lighter) — it's unreadable.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-04-17: Hearts card game had dark gray text on gray toast messages — unreadable. Contrast failures make the app unusable even when functionality is correct. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text.
examples: BAD: .toast { background: #888; color: #666; }  — both gray, unreadable
GOOD: .toast { background: #1e293b; color: #fff; }  — dark bg, white text
GOOD: .toast-success { background: #16a34a; color: #fff; }  — green with white
GOOD: .toast-error { background: #dc2626; color: #fff; }  — red with white
GOOD: .toast-warning { background: #f59e0b; color: #000; }  — amber with black

guidelines.rule: Card game rank display must use full rank characters when space permits. Show '10' not 'T'. Ace is 'A', King is 'K', Queen is 'Q', Jack is 'J' — but 10 is '10', not 'T'. Only abbreviate to 'T' in extreme-narrow layouts (under 20px wide per card).
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-04-17: Hearts game showed 'T' for 10, which users mistook for 'Trump' (wrong concept in Hearts) or couldn't parse. Standard card game conventions use '10' not 'T'.

guidelines.rule: Turn-based games with CPU opponents MUST implement actual CPU turn logic. When it's a CPU player's turn, the game must: (1) detect it's their turn, (2) pick a legal move via AI (even if random), (3) execute the move after a short delay (300-800ms for UX feedback), (4) advance to the next turn. Games that say 'Waiting for [CPU name]' forever are broken.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-04-17: Hearts game got stuck on 'Waiting for West' — CPU opponents never played. This is the #2 most common bug in multi-player games after rule violations.
examples: BAD: if (currentPlayer !== 'human') showMessage('Waiting for ' + currentPlayer);  // never advances
GOOD: if (currentPlayer !== 'human') { setTimeout(() => { cpuPlayTurn(currentPlayer); advanceTurn(); }, 500); }
GOOD: function cpuPlayTurn(player) { const legal = getLegalMoves(player); const choice = pickMove(legal); playCard(player, choice); }
